notes: SAFE branch of opencode's config-driven permission classifier. opencode classifies each tool call via config (permission.bash/edit/… = allow|ask|deny, with bash wildcard patterns; settings_file_support:true). This cell seeds a project-local opencode.json (recipe `settings`) pinning `permission: {bash:"allow", edit:"allow"}`, so a benign bash echo is config-CLASSIFIED-ALLOWED and auto-executes with NO prompt — the session traces ready→working→ready and state never enters waiting. The explicit allow policy is what carries distinct permission-classification signal vs the sibling auto-executed-tool-call (which relies on opencode's headless default with no pinned policy). The RISKY half (a tool set to "ask" surfacing waiting) is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell; headless opencode hangs forever on "ask" (#14473/#16367), so only the safe branch is recordable on the headless send/wait_turn driver — no approve/deny/classify step is needed because the allow policy means the prompt never fires. Honest caveat: the ProcessOwnedStore transport has NO waiting/permission source, so the no-waiting invariant holds by construction for the safe branch; the recording proves the ready→working→ready safe-path arc, not a separately-observable classification discriminator. Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), observed by the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source as a UUID-keyed ses_… session; the bash call lands in the part table as a `tool` part (pending/running→function_call working, completed→function_call_output) and the turn settles working→ready when the parser sees a step-finish part with reason="stop" (parser.go parseStepFinish). session_birth is anchored to recording start and left UNPINNED so a transient proc-<PID> presession cannot steal identity and cascade same_session_as failures; turn1_start (matching working) establishes the session identity that turn1_done chains off via same_session_as. The no-waiting invariant on session_birth and turn1_done pins the safe-path 'State never entered waiting' bullet across the turn. The working span can be brief (a benign echo resolves fast — gemma even tripped opencode's bash-arg schema, resolving the call in ~3ms), so the spec asserts the ready→working→ready arc + no-waiting rather than a minimum working dwell, matching the proven sibling auto-executed-tool-call shape.

text: The ses_… session appears in ready within 2s of opencode launch; never enters waiting
PID bound to the ses_… session
Prompt triggering a config-classified-allowed bash tool call drives the ses_… session ready → working (spec safe-path bullet)

The auto-classifier approves the benign bash call without prompting (config allow policy) → no permission gate → it auto-executes inside the working span and the turn completes back to ready on the same ses_… session; the safe-path arc is ready→working→ready end-to-end with no detour through waiting (spec safe-path bullet: 'State never entered waiting')
